items: The “constant-time” and energy claims omit critical system components (laser sources, detectors/ADCs, interconnects, repeat passes for multiple layers), potentially overstating practical throughput and underestimating power.
The demonstrated experiment validates only a DONN kernel, not the full ONE stack (including XBAR and nonlinear stages), leaving system-level feasibility and integration challenges unaddressed.
Nonlinear operations are electronic; the cumulative opto-electronic conversion overhead, latencies, and synchronization are not modeled in end-to-end performance estimates.

Reported comparisons emphasize training/validation loss rather than standardized test metrics (e.g., relative L2, nRMSE) with error bars and multiple random seeds; some figures compare losses across models without equalized training protocols, which may limit fairness.
There is limited ablation to isolate the contributions of the Fourier branch, real-space branch, and physics-parameter conditioning.
Generalization properties central to operator learning (cross-resolution, geometry variation, boundary-condition shifts, temporal extrapolation) are not systematically evaluated.
Error definitions and scales vary across tasks (absolute, normalized, relative), making cross-task comparisons hard; a unified metric suite would help.

Data generation details (boundary conditions, coefficient distributions, solver discretizations, time-stepping choices) are insufficient for exact reproducibility or for assessing distribution shift robustness.

The discussion of neural-operator baselines is incomplete relative to recent spectral/multiscale/attentional advances (e.g., hierarchical attentive FNOs, DPNO, NSM). Stronger baselines and discussion are warranted, especially for multiscale and time-dependent problems.

Related “O(1)-in-time” neural surrogates conditioned on timestep and parameters (e.g., time-conditioned EM surrogates) are relevant to the constant-time narrative and could better contextualize ONE’s claims.
The self-supervised update with pseudo-labels risks confirmation bias and error accumulation; formal safeguards, error detectors, or theoretical guarantees are not provided.
Confidence weighting appears internally inconsistent: the provided formula seems to up-weight low-confidence experts; the definition of “confidence” may also be flawed (see detailed comments).
Scalability to higher spatial DoF, grayscale inputs, or phase-sensitive scenarios is not demonstrated; experiments focus on binary 16×16 and 32×32 patterns.

Comparisons are limited primarily to a single StaticNN baseline; missing evaluations against other adaptive strategies (e.g., periodic TM/RVITM refresh, vectorial time reversal refresh, configuration training, dynamic synthesis networks, continual-learning baselines).
Ablations are sparse: no systematic study of update interval, forgetting schedule, router/gating alternatives, or sensitivity to pseudo-label noise is provided.
Update-time latency and compute budget per 5 s batch are not reported, leaving unclear whether the system is truly real-time in practice.
The confidence metric and weighting equations appear contradictory or erroneous (details below).
The “rebuild/copy” schedule for short-term experts is under-specified (what is copied, from which expert, and with what initialization?).
The video compression ratio is reported as 1.39‰, which is unconventional and hard to interpret without bitrate/throughput context.
Limited engagement with recent adaptive or physics-driven dynamic compensation methods (e.g., time-averaged TM/fluctuation eigenchannels, adjoint optimization in dynamic media).
The relationship to (and potential complementarity with) RVITM-type fast intensity-only characterization and SmoothGS single-iteration TM recovery is not experimentally explored.

Reliance on the local-phase (locally periodic) approximation and RCWA unit-cell modeling leaves inter-cell coupling and high-NA effects unaccounted for; this can reduce predictive fidelity for NA≈0.45.
The computational backend uses an INR-based semi-blind deconvolution, which is comparatively heavy-weight despite being termed “simple”; real-time feasibility and robustness are not quantified.
The design objective samples only two incident angles (0° and 10°); FOV performance and angular aberration control are not comprehensively addressed.
Quantitative metrics such as measured MTFs across wavelengths/field angles, focusing/transmission efficiencies, and SNR/throughput are sparse in the main text.
Fairness and parity of the computational reconstruction across different optics (same priors/hyperparameters/initialization) are not fully detailed; this matters for comparing image quality.
PSF measurements are confined to 8.23–10.93 μm; 12 μm behavior is inferred via filtered imaging but not directly measured with the same PSF protocol.

Several inconsistencies in reported device parameters and sampling: period Λ=4 μm vs. Λ=10 μm; aperture 1 cm vs. statements implying 2.5 cm or 10 cm; “403 × 101” RCWA simulations appears to be 40^3 × 101; “8 optimized wavelengths” vs. “13 wavelengths during optimization.” These should be reconciled.
The term “simple computational backend” understates the sophistication of the INR deconvolution; clarify terminology.

Limited discussion of closely related hybrid optical-computational LWIR efforts (e.g., hybrid meta-refractive correction), multiwavelength multilayer metasurfaces, and prior visible-band inverse-designed EDOF metasurfaces that use similar multi-scale differentiable pipelines.
No baseline comparison with forward EDOF masks (e.g., cubic/log-asphere) in LWIR, or with MDLs that have shown broadband LWIR imaging.

Reliance on the point-dipole model (PDM) to invert near-field signals into dielectric function and radiative rates introduces model dependence; sensitivity to tip geometry, tapping amplitude, and tip–sample distance is not fully quantified.
The Lorentzian parametrization conflates dephasing and nonradiative channels; the identifiability of radiative rate versus εbg and thickness d is not rigorously analyzed.
The screening length is concluded to be <20 nm but limited by spatial resolution; no deconvolution or tip-spread function analysis is attempted to refine this bound.
Twist-angle determination via SHG leaves a 30° ambiguity (15° vs 45°), and potential contributions from local strain or doping to the observed redshifts are not systematically excluded.
Parameter maps (ω0, γ, εbg) across the flakes are not shown, despite the capability of hyperspectral s-SNOM to reveal spatial inhomogeneity.

Notation in the Lorentzian model and Table 1 contains minor inconsistencies/typos (e.g., symbols for rates “Yr,o”, “Ynr+Yd”) that could confuse readers; εbg values and their physical meaning could be better contextualized.
The connection between phase contrast and absorption is cited but would benefit from a brief intuitive explanation tailored to the s-SNOM observable used.

Quantitative inversion approaches and harmonic-depth sensitivity analyses in visible s-SNOM (e.g., dual-harmonic inversion/inference methods) are not discussed; comparison to complementary far-field ellipsometry or reflectance on similar flakes is missing.
Recent near-field works on exciton–plasmon polaritons and cryogenic s-SNOM resolving negative ε1 regions provide useful context for the damping-limited room-temperature regime discussed here.
Key performance metrics (NEP, specific detectivity D*, noise spectra, response time/bandwidth, dynamic range, linearity) are not reported, limiting assessment against state-of-the-art.
Stability and retention of the ferroelectric domain pattern under device operation (temperature, humidity, optical/thermal cycling) are not quantified.
Angular/polarization dependence and tolerance of the plasmonic resonance are not characterized; incident angle sensitivity could impact practical imaging.
Uniformity, yield, and variability across pixels/channels are not addressed; nanoscale domain width control and its spread are critical for arrays.
The imaging results are simulated from commercial camera data rather than produced by a physically integrated multichannel array with on-chip readout, weakening system-level claims.
The relation between Raman G-band shifts and EF is treated simplistically; potential confounds from strain/temperature are not fully disentangled or calibrated.
The attribution of channel selectivity solely to plasmonic coupling would benefit from polarization controls, thermal background subtraction, and mechanism isolation (e.g., PTE vs PV vs bolometric).
The description of the transmission measurement using an AFM probe is unconventional and under-explained, raising questions about spot size, alignment, and sampling uniformity.
The “6-channel-pixel” physical layout and how six domain-widths are co-located within one pixel are not fully clarified; implications for fill factor and optical crosstalk are unclear.
Benchmarks against leading room-temperature MIR detectors (e.g., vdW b-AsP heterojunctions) in terms of D*, NEP, and speed are absent, despite comparable reported responsivities.
Comparison against prior graphene plasmonic detectors (nanopatterned, grating-coupled, or substrate-patterned) lacks a quantitative head-to-head with noise, uniformity, and scalability metrics.
The deep-learning imaging gains are not contrasted with software-only baselines (e.g., edge-preserving filters or multi-scale CNNs) on the same single-channel inputs.
